description:
GOAL: Cooperative scheduler with processes-as-continuations. Single OS thread for v1.

## Decisions captured (from design discussion)

- ONE shared address space across processes (NOT BEAM-style separate spaces). Pointers valid across process boundaries.
- Per-process heap chosen specifically for BOUNDED GC PAUSES: each process's heap is GC'd independently; other processes keep running.
- CPS codegen (from .11) makes processes trivial: a process IS a continuation pointer + a per-process heap. No stackful coroutines, no corosensei, no fiber stacks. Switching processes = update which continuation the trampoline drives next.
- Cooperative: yields at receive (and possibly at allocation safepoints for fairness — TBD).
- Single OS thread for v1. Multi-thread / work-stealing later.

## Deliverables (new src/runtime/process.rs and src/runtime/scheduler.rs)

```rust
pub struct Process {
    pid: PidId,
    next_frame: *mut HeapHeader,  // current continuation; null = exited
    heap: Heap,                    // private per-process heap
    mailbox: VecDeque<FzValue>,    // .19.3
    state: State,                  // Ready / Blocked(receive) / Exited
}

pub struct Scheduler {
    procs: HashMap<PidId, Process>,
    run_queue: VecDeque<PidId>,
    next_pid: u32,
}

impl Scheduler {
    pub fn new() -> Self;
    pub fn spawn_initial(&mut self, entry_frame: *mut HeapHeader) -> PidId;
    pub fn run_until_idle(&mut self);  // drives trampoline across all ready procs
}
```

The trampoline now picks the current process from scheduler, runs ITS continuation one step (or until it yields), then switches.

## Tests

- Spawn 3 processes, each running a counter loop; verify all make progress.
- A process that exits (returns Halt) is removed from scheduler.
- Single-process degenerate case: existing JIT tests still work (one process named "main").

## Risks

- Cooperative-only means a tight loop without yield can monopolize the scheduler. v1 acceptable; preemption (reductions counter) is a follow-up.
- Mailbox + receive (.19.3) introduces scheduler ↔ process interaction (block / wake); design here must accommodate.

## Out of scope

- spawn / self / pid (.19.2 — depends on this infra)
- Mailbox + send / receive (.19.3)
- Multi-threaded scheduler (follow-up)
- Preemption via reductions counter (follow-up)
title: Process / scheduler infrastructure (process = continuation ptr)
